tweets.text: CLARITY does the opposite of what anti-tech Dems claim. Under their direction, the SEC refused to create rules and that led to the worst frauds and scams in the industry's history. Now, they're trying to stop CLARITY—leaving consumers exposed for their own political gain.
tweets.summary: Crypto policy advocate defends CLARITY Act as needed market-structure rulemaking against Democratic opposition.

tweets.text: Moderate democrats shouldn't fear @SenWarren's “anti-crypto army”. Why? Because it does not exist. Pass CLARITY. https://t.co/JMiQrMt2r1
tweets.summary: Crypto policy advocate argues Warren's 'anti-crypto army' is overstated, urges Congress to pass the CLARITY Act.

tweets.text: "Good idea, but where those tokens go to, if you randomly distribute them again, you can end up back at the same problems," @DrNickA on Alex Van De Sande's proposal to redelegate 5 million ENS tokens to reform the DAO https://t.co/hTPZWV3aAJ
tweets.summary: Analyst critiques ENS proposal to redelegate 5M tokens, warning redistribution could recreate the same governance problems.

tweets.text: RT @lordvolth: We often hear that TradFi is "skeptical" of blockchains and DeFi... in reality it's just discipline. These institutions aren't waiting to believe in the tech. They're just telling us a COGS story. @cc_crowley and I talk about the near-term vs. long-term opportunity in programmable financial infrastructure 👇 Capital Markets players are adopting exactly the primitives that improve unit economics: atomic settlement, programmable money, tokenized collateral. Decentralization, the part with no line item, maybe gets left behind? CLARITY would be a real step toward the "elegant convergence" everyone predicts. But there's a long window where the short game and the long game both matter. Not competing bets, but parallel ones. Near-term, builders can help institutions adopt the primitives that move more capital onchain. Long-term, an open system continues to evolve and push the innovations that may eventually subsume the old system. @a16zcrypto https://t.co/do9dblLknZ
tweets.summary: TradFi adopts DeFi primitives (atomic settlement, tokenized collateral); CLARITY Act seen as step toward crypto-TradFi convergence.

tweets.text: "The stablecoin requirements under Genius conflict with the requirements under MiCA, which is candidly nonsensical considering the whole point of crypto and stablecoins" Katherine on the contradiction between Genius and MiCA that's frustrating global crypto companies "The big difference here is they want to cover tokenized assets more generally, but they also want to cover stablecoins issued by non-EU stablecoin issuers. One source of frustration for anyone that works at a global crypto company is that the stablecoin requirements under Genius conflict with the requirements under MiCA" "You have a slew of stablecoin issuers with either a single issuance model, I believe Circle has single issuance, versus a multi-issuance model like MoonPay, which has multiple entities in different jurisdictions issuing different stables. What MiCA is now saying is, sorry US and non-EU stablecoin issuers, you have to get in line, you're going to have comparable standards to the EU issuers" "Everyone has a few months, till September I think, to provide input. So we won't see proposed regulatory revisions for at least a year probably. But what's happening in my mind is that Europe is kind of freaking out in response to the growth of tokenization and stables, and they're figuring out how to fix this"
tweets.summary: GENIUS Act and EU MiCA stablecoin rules conflict, forcing non-EU issuers (Circle, MoonPay) toward EU-comparable standards.

tweets.text: It’s actually pretty cool that MetaDAO keeps iterating. This raise seems pretty decent. Sure there’s previous investors but there’s no unlocks for a year, and in a year it should be clear whether they are successful (at which point it’s sustainable) or going to zero. Way better than the Ranger terms that instantly PvPed prior investors against ICO participants via instant unlock dilution. The business as is likely has some residual value vs it being a pure gamble. Even if there’s no 100x outcome (unlikely given payments is very winner takes all), there’s pretty decent exit potential by selling to a larger player, which is nice. Most interesting bit would be how an exit might happen. Acquisition of payment customer lists often have earn-outs attached or might be equity roll ups, which adds complication to a non-moonshot exit. The existence of a public market valuation also affects any M&A activity but presumably a “take-private” proposal could happen. One thing I remain somewhat unsure about on all MetaDAO raises is, ironically, how the project team is making money. PTSD from being founder rug pulled seems to result in retail investors loving when the project team itself has very restricted economics but Id rather the team be more economically aligned. Selling 75% of the economics at this point, with the remainder being locked behind aggressive price targets seems a bit much, given most Series A rounds only give up 30%ish. I also think futarchy really isn’t a thing for early stage projects and MetaDAO might end up just making them governance tokens with a futarchy conversion only after a project hits hard escape velocity. Might ape this one to see what happens tbh. I wonder what’s the mechanism for selling to VCs, a holdback of some ICO tokens?
tweets.summary: Analysis of MetaDAO's token/equity capital structure, unlock terms, and futarchy governance model.

tweets.text: RT @SenatorHagerty: America is primed for digital asset leadership. The GENIUS Act has already helped unlock investment and innovation in the stablecoin sector. Now, we have an opportunity to strengthen America’s leadership in digital assets, but only if we pass the CLARITY Act. The time is now, let’s get it done. 🇺🇸

tweets.text: No rule can give the CFTC spot authority over digital assets, grant new sanctions authority against adversaries, or protect developers from unwarranted prosecution. Only Congress can. The Clarity Act is the only path that works.
tweets.summary: Sen. Lummis argues only Congress via the Clarity Act can grant CFTC spot authority over digital assets.

tweets.text: RT @LorenzoARK: The Robinhood Chain is the cleanest case study of what happened to ETH's economics over time. Since inception, @RobinhoodApp Chain has grossed ~$816K in revenue. @Arbitrum, the middleware provider, takes 10%: ~$80K. Arbitrum then pays Ethereum for settlement: $1,538. The margin profile roughly: Robinhood: 89% Arbitrum: 10% Ethereum: 0.15% If your thesis is "ETH is money," Robinhood building here is ultra bullish. More activity, more ETH collateral, more lindyness. If your thesis is "ETH is a revenue generating asset," this is the ultra-bear case. And here's the uncomfortable truth: Robinhood was never going to build on Solana, Sui or any monolithic L1. They want the stack customization. They want to be landlords, not renters. Ethereum won this deal on merit. It's just not pricing it right. A healthy split to me looks more like: Robinhood: 75% Arbitrum: 10% Ethereum: 15% Ethereum sells the most valuable settlement layer in crypto at marginal cost. Things need to change. @ethlabs_org
tweets.summary: Analysis of Robinhood's Arbitrum-based chain deal and revenue split implications for Ethereum's economics.

tweets.text: RT @FLEOAORG: FLEOA has submitted a letter to the U.S. Senate Committee on Banking, Housing, and Urban Affairs expressing its support for H.R. 3633, the Digital Asset Market Clarity Act, while offering targeted recommendations to strengthen the legislation and preserve essential federal law enforcement authorities. Read our full press release about this here: https://t.co/K4CHWM9AhY #FLEOA #PressRelease #Legislation
